title: Norman Statement
orientation: The Norman Statement is a confessional document of eleven articles that codifies the theological and strategic distinctives of the Abolitionist Movement, articulating affirmations and denials on the gospel, the Church, Scripture, immediatism, equal protection, higher law, and the rejection of pro-life incrementalism. It functions as the doctrinal basis for the Abolitionists Rising network and any organization wishing to identify with the movement.
audiences: Pastor or church elder weighing the document

questions.question: Why does the Norman Statement insist that fighting abortion must be explicitly gospel-centered rather than purely a humanitarian or secular cause?
questions.answer: Because the statement defines abortion as fundamentally sin (specifically the sin of child sacrifice) which arises from suppression of the truth, the drafters argue that only the gospel of Jesus Christ can address the root cause. Activism detached from the gospel is therefore deemed insufficient, and activism centered on a false gospel is denied the label 'abolitionist' altogether.
questions.quote: WE DENY that anti-abortion activism detached from the gospel and a Christian worldview is sufficient to address abortion because abortion is, fundamentally, sin resulting from suppression of the truth.⁷ We deny that activism centered on a false gospel is abolitionist in nature.

questions.question: What does the statement say should be the primary institution leading the work of abolition, and what does it warn against?
questions.audience: Pastor or church elder weighing the document
questions.answer: Article III asserts that abolition is to be led by the body of Christ—the Church—expressed in local churches, and that no political party, parachurch ministry, or abolitionist organization should be the primary driving force. It also rebukes churches that remain silent in the midst of what it calls a holocaust.
questions.quote: WE DENY that any political party, parachurch ministry, or abolitionist organization should be the primary driving force behind the Abolitionist Movement.⁷ We deny that God approves of any church that remains silent and refuses to act in the midst of a holocaust.

questions.question: How does the Norman Statement frame pro-life incremental legislation, and why does it reject it?
questions.answer: Article V brands incremental legislation that regulates and thereby allows abortion as an 'iniquitous decree,' arguing it forfeits the moral standard needed to combat abortion and serves as a distraction from genuine repentance. The drafters summarize the position with the maxim that incrementalism in theory is perpetuity in practice.
questions.quote: We affirm that incremental legislation which regulates and thereby allows abortion is an iniquitous decree.³ We affirm that pro-life legislation is often used as a substitute that distracts Christians from the call for abolition and allows pro-life civil magistrates to be celebrated for doing less than they are commanded by God in Scripture.

questions.question: Does the statement claim immediate abolition is guaranteed if the Church demands it?
questions.answer: No. While the statement insists that the demand must be for immediate abolition without compromise, it explicitly denies that demanding immediacy guarantees immediate results, acknowledging that national repentance and abolition usually take time and is not guaranteed for any nation.
questions.quote: We deny that our demand for immediate abolition means that the goal will necessarily be achieved immediately because the work of bringing about national repentance and abolition usually takes time and is not guaranteed for any nation.

questions.question: What is the statement's position on whether mothers who procure abortions should be subject to prosecution?
questions.answer: Article VII denies that mothers should receive blanket statutory immunity, arguing that such partiality is unjust and enables a self-managed abortion industry. It also affirms that varying circumstances should be weighed by prosecutors, judges, and juries on a case-by-case basis, and that anyone who knowingly and willfully participates in killing a preborn child is guilty.
questions.quote: WE DENY that the mother of the preborn child should be given blanket statutory immunity from prosecution in the killing of her child because this partiality is unjust and enables a self-managed abortion industry.

questions.question: What constitutional and biblical grounds does the document offer for criminalizing abortion as homicide?
questions.answer: Article VII grounds equal protection in two pillars: Scripture's repeated prohibitions of partiality, which the drafters say forbid classifying abortion as anything less than homicide, and the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, which they argue prohibits states from treating abortion differently from other homicides.
questions.quote: WE AFFIRM that abortion is murder and must be criminalized as such, that preborn children are image-bearers who deserve the exact same legal protection afforded to everyone else,¹ that scripture’s repeated prohibitions of partiality² prohibit the legal classification of abortion as anything other than homicide, and that the equal protection clause of the 14th amendment of the U.S. Constitution prohibits states from classifying abortion as anything other than homicide.

questions.question: What does the statement say a lesser civil magistrate should do when a higher authority commands compliance with abortion laws?
questions.answer: Article VIII teaches the doctrine of the lesser magistrate: when a lesser official must choose between obeying a higher magistrate or obeying God, he must follow the higher Law of God, and he should defy the higher magistrate whenever necessary to abolish abortion. The statement further declares that compliance with any future federal pro-abortion ruling would be a sin worthy of church discipline.
questions.quote: We affirm that whenever it is necessary for a lesser magistrate to defy a higher magistrate in order to abolish abortion, the lesser magistrate should do so.⁴ We affirm that every state and federal governing official who complied with Roe v. Wade between 1973-2022 sinned in so doing. We affirm that compliance with any future pro-abortion ruling, order, or law of the federal government would be a sin worthy of church discipline.

questions.question: Why does the Norman Statement insist on linguistic separation between 'abolitionist' and 'pro-life'?
questions.answer: Article IX argues that careful linguistic differentiation is strategically necessary because the Pro-Life Movement is so adrift in secularism and compromise that conflating the two blurs the line between sound and false doctrine and hinders necessary repentance. The drafters deny this is about tribalism or branding for any particular organization.
questions.quote: We affirm that careful linguistic differentiation between abolitionist and pro-life ideas, organizations, bills, and candidates is necessary to the existence of the Abolitionist Movement as a separate entity from the compromised Pro-Life Movement and helpful to the success of abolitionist initiatives and efforts.

questions.question: Does the statement consider abolishing abortion to be part of the gospel itself?
questions.answer: No. Article I carefully distinguishes that abolishing abortion is not the content of the gospel, but rather an implication of it and a Great Commission issue, in which Christians are commanded to teach people to obey all that Christ has commanded. Article XI separately defines the gospel proper as Christ's person and work for the salvation of sinners.
questions.quote: We affirm that abolishing abortion, while not the content of the gospel (see Article XI), is an implication of the gospel, thus, a Great Commission issue, wherein Christians are charged to teach people to obey all that Christ has commanded⁶ (See Article X).

questions.question: How does the statement view the role of public polling and political pragmatism in shaping the movement's strategy?
questions.answer: Article II rejects worldly pragmatism as both unfaithful to Scripture and ineffective for lasting change. It explicitly denies that public polling or political winds should dictate what Christians say or do in the political sphere, instead grounding strategy in obedience to God whose sovereignty governs results.
questions.quote: WE DENY that worldly-wise pragmatism is faithful to Scripture, and that it can achieve real, lasting social and political change. We deny that public polling or the political winds should dictate what Christians say and do in the political sphere.

questions.question: What does the statement say about America's standing before God and what is required to avert judgment?
questions.audience: Pastor or church elder weighing the document
questions.answer: Article VI declares the United States is under God's judgment, evidenced by widespread acceptance of child sacrifice, and that the only remedy is national repentance, which must include forsaking sin, prayer, fasting, solemn assemblies, and bearing fruit through the establishment of justice for preborn children. The statement denies that any substitute for national repentance can avert national judgment and ruin.
questions.quote: We deny that the United States has any special status that would exempt us from God’s judgment.⁵ We deny that there is any effective substitute for national repentance that would avert national judgment and ruin.

questions.question: What stance does the Norman Statement take on the sufficiency of secular argumentation in the abortion debate?
questions.answer: Article IV denies that politics is a neutral sphere in which God's Word is not binding, and further denies that secular argumentation alone is sufficient to address abortion, on the grounds that secular reasoning cannot ultimately justify objective truth or human rights.
questions.quote: WE DENY that politics is a neutral sphere where the Word of God is not binding.⁸ We deny that secular argumentation alone is sufficient to address the sin of abortion because secular reasoning cannot provide a justification for objective truth or human rights.

questions.question: Who drafted the Norman Statement and what kinds of organizations do they represent?
questions.answer: The drafters listed include pastors and leaders such as T. Russell Hunter (founder of Abolitionists Rising), Dusty Deevers (Grace Reformed Baptist Church of Elgin), Bill Ascol (Founders Ministries board member), Brett Baggett (Ekklesia Muskogee), Austin Beigel (End Abortion Ohio), Wes Scroggins (Abolish Abortion Missouri), and many others connected to state-level abolish-abortion organizations and Reformed Baptist churches.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Develops Article VI's theme of national repentance as a non-negotiable component of abolition.

related.title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Defends the equal-protection / no-blanket-immunity position laid out in Article VII.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Directly expounds Article IX's call for linguistic and ideological separation from the Pro-Life Movement.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Argues the case made in Article V against pro-life incrementalism as iniquitous and ineffective.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Theological grounding for the immediatist stance affirmed in Article V.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Engages prominent critics with the same anti-compromise theology that the Norman Statement codifies.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Applies Articles V and VIII (immediatism and higher law / lesser magistrate doctrine) to the post-Roe legal landscape.
